Totems will be strategically placed along high-traffic corridors and decision points to guide pedestrian flow toward priority sites, including stadiums, fan festival locations, and transit hubs. The program includes the design, fabrication, permitting, and installation of engineered totem structures, with consideration for accessibility, visibility, durability, and integration within the existing urban environment.

In addition, this RoDAR was prepared in the same manner previously approved under RoDAR RD042026-27, which the exact submission. At the time, the use of Form 4 was accepted without issue.

Toronto-TMC: - This project involves two sequential phases of underground utility work around Innis College on the University of Toronto St. George campus. The scope includes the installation of a chilled water system and a hydro duct bank, coordinated to avoid conflicts with existing and future infrastructure, as shown in the attached utility drawings.

Phase 1:
- Location: bPNichol Lane (between Sussex Avenue and Lane South Bloor Street East, in the vicinity of Innis College).
- Scope of Work: Installation of chilled water lines approximately 30 meters North of Sussex Avenue, including connections and terminations as per design drawings. The chilled water piping will be installed in a shared utility trench with consideration for future heating line installations by the University.

Impact:
- Partial closure of bPNichol Lane. The entire lane will not be blocked, but access in the work zone around Innis College will be restricted.

column22: Toronto-TMC: The wayfinding totem program is designed to support intuitive navigation for visitors across key routes and destinations, enhancing the overall event experience while aligning with the City’s Look & Wayfinding strategy. Totems will be strategically placed along high-traffic corridors and decision points to guide pedestrian flow toward priority sites, including stadiums, fan festival locations, and transit hubs. The program includes the design, fabrication, permitting, and installation of engineered totem structures, with consideration for accessibility, visibility, durability, and integration within the existing urban environment.

column22: Toronto-TMC: The Broadview and Eastern Flood Protection (BEFP) project will address flood vulnerability for urban land east of the Don River, between Eastern Avenue and rail corridor, the existing underpass at Eastern Avenue, and the proposed underpass at Broadview Avenue. As part of this construction, there will be utility relocations and reconstruction in this area, including city storm and sanitary sewers that run along Sunlight Park Rd. Buildings related to one business within the project footprint will be removed to allow for construction of the FPL and other necessary local infrastructure. The removal also enables future projects by others such as the Broadview Avenue Extension, East Harbour Transit Hub & Development and the Ontario Line.

Toronto-TMC: The construction of the New Etobicoke Civic Centre.

Multiplex Construction Canada will be managing the construction of the New Etobicoke Civic Centre Located at 3755 Bloor Street W. Etobicoke, Ontario M9A 1A2, located within the newly redeveloped Etobicoke Civic Centre Precinct (formerly the Westwood Theatre Lands).

The ECC is a mixed-use building comprising a wide range of community focused program and services. The building is arranged in a series of tall volumes towards the north, housing the municipal offices, while the lower floors towards the south contain all the public functions. It will also be compliant to the Toronto Accessibility Design Guidelines (TADG) to extend greater accessibility for all users.

With 508,000 square feet of accessible public-facing space, the new civic centre is the first of its kind to be built in the last 50 years and is designed as a state-of-the-art mixed-used civic hub that includes:
